graph_vector_fulltext.message:
I don’t have enough information in the provided context to trace an “enhanced human oversight” requirement or to explain its interaction with a “law enforcement exception for dual verification,” nor to confirm a specific “prohibition on sole-output decisions,” because those elements are not contained in the excerpts available here.

From the context I can trace these points on biometric identification (especially for law enforcement and post-remote use):

- Processing biometric data for law-enforcement biometric identification must comply with Article 10 of Directive (EU) 2016/680: it is allowed only where “strictly necessary,” subject to “appropriate safeguards” for the rights and freedoms of the data subject, and must be authorised by Union or Member State law. It must also respect the principles in Article 4(1) of Directive (EU) 2016/680 (lawfulness, fairness, transparency, purpose limitation, storage limitation, accuracy).
- Post-remote biometric identification systems should be subject to safeguards and “always” used in a way that is proportionate, legitimate and strictly necessary, and thus targeted:
  - targeted to the individuals to be identified,
  - targeted to the location,
  - targeted to the temporal scope,
  - based on a “closed data set of legally acquired video footage.”
- In any case, post-remote biometric identification “should not be used” in the framework of law enforcement to lead to “indiscriminate surveillance,” and the conditions for post-remote biometric identification should not be used to circumvent the prohibition and strict exceptions for real-time remote biometric identification.

If you can provide the specific section/page of the AI Act text you’re referencing for (1) enhanced human oversight, (2) dual verification in law enforcement, and (3) bans on sole-output decisions, I can connect them precisely to the post-remote safeguards quoted above and map the full chain.
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_question: For biometric identification systems, trace the enhanced human oversight requirement, its interaction with the law enforcement exception for dual verification, the prohibition on sole-output decisions, and the post-remote biometric identification safeguards.

From the context I can trace these points on biometric identification (especially for law enforcement and post-remote use):

- Processing biometric data for law-enforcement biometric identification must comply with Article 10 of Directive (EU) 2016/680: it is allowed only where “strictly necessary,” subject to “appropriate safeguards” for the rights and freedoms of the data subject, and must be authorised by Union or Member State law. It must also respect the principles in Article 4(1) of Directive (EU) 2016/680 (lawfulness, fairness, transparency, purpose limitation, storage limitation, accuracy).
- Post-remote biometric identification systems should be subject to safeguards and “always” used in a way that is proportionate, legitimate and strictly necessary, and thus targeted:
  - targeted to the individuals to be identified,
  - targeted to the location,
  - targeted to the temporal scope,
  - based on a “closed data set of legally acquired video footage.”
- In any case, post-remote biometric identification “should not be used” in the framework of law enforcement to lead to “indiscriminate surveillance,” and the conditions for post-remote biometric identification should not be used to circumvent the prohibition and strict exceptions for real-time remote biometric identification.

Content: Text Content:
94) Any processing of biometric data involved in the use of AI systems for biometric identification for the purpose of  law enforcement needs to comply with Article 10 of Directive (EU) 2016/680, that allows such processing only  where strictly necessary, subject to appropriate safeguards for the rights and freedoms of the data subject, and where  authorised by Union or Member State law. Such use, when authorised, also needs to respect the principles laid down  in Article 4 (1) of Directive (
----
ometric identification systems, the use of post-remote biometric  identification systems should be subject to safeguards. Post-remote biometric identification systems should always be  used in a way that is proportionate, legitimate and strictly necessary, and thus targeted, in terms of the individuals to  be identified, the location, temporal scope and based on a closed data set of legally acquired video footage. In any  case, post-remote biometric identification systems should not be used in the framework of law enforcement
----
  case, post-remote biometric identification systems should not be used in the framework of law enforcement to lead  to indiscriminate surveillance. The conditions for post-remote biometric identification should in any case not  provide a basis to circumvent the conditions of the prohibition and strict exceptions for real time remote biometric  identification. (96) In order to efficiently ensure that fundamental rights are protected, deployers of high-risk AI systems that are bodies  governed by public law, or private entities providing
----
guaranteeing the uniform protection of overriding reasons of public interest and of rights of persons throughout the  internal market on the basis of Article 114 of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union (TFEU). To the  extent that this Regulation contains specific rules on the protection of individuals with regard to the processing of  personal data concerning restrictions of the use of AI systems for remote biometric identification for the purpose of  law enforcement, of the use of AI systems for risk assessments of natural
----

chunks.text: 94) Any processing of biometric data involved in the use of AI systems for biometric identification for the purpose of law enforcement needs to comply with Article 10 of Directive (EU) 2016/680, that allows such processing only where strictly necessary, subject to appropriate safeguards for the rights and freedoms of the data subject, and where authorised by Union or Member State law. Such use, when authorised, also needs to respect the principles laid down in Article 4 (1) of Directive (

ometric identification systems, the use of post-remote biometric identification systems should be subject to safeguards. Post-remote biometric identification systems should always be used in a way that is proportionate, legitimate and strictly necessary, and thus targeted, in terms of the individuals to be identified, the location, temporal scope and based on a closed data set of legally acquired video footage. In any case, post-remote biometric identification systems should not be used in the framework of law enforcement

case, post-remote biometric identification systems should not be used in the framework of law enforcement to lead to indiscriminate surveillance. The conditions for post-remote biometric identification should in any case not provide a basis to circumvent the conditions of the prohibition and strict exceptions for real time remote biometric identification. (96) In order to efficiently ensure that fundamental rights are protected, deployers of high-risk AI systems that are bodies governed by public law, or private entities providing

guaranteeing the uniform protection of overriding reasons of public interest and of rights of persons throughout the internal market on the basis of Article 114 of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union (TFEU).
